schools.summary: Paul, traveling the Egnatian Way through Amphipolis and Apollonia, came to Thessalonica, the chief Macedonian city, where there was a synagogue — his regular starting-point for reasoning from the Scriptures with Jews and God-fearers.

themes: passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia
came to Thessalonica, where was a synagogue
Paul's regular method
the opening of the Thessalonian ministry

schools.summary: Paul, as his custom was, entered the synagogue and over three sabbaths reasoned from the Scriptures — his regular method of beginning with the Jews (Robertson), disputing and discoursing from the Old Testament (Calvin), the common ground; grounding the gospel in the Scriptures.

themes: as his manner was, went in unto them
three sabbath days reasoned out of the scriptures
Paul's scriptural method
reasoning from the Old Testament

schools.summary: Paul opened (expounded) and alleged (set forth) from the Scriptures that the Christ must suffer and rise, and that Jesus is that Christ — proving the necessity of the Messiah's suffering and rising (contrary to Jewish expectation) from the prophets, and identifying Jesus as that suffering and risen Messiah.

themes: opening and alleging
Christ must needs have suffered, and risen again
this Jesus is Christ
the suffering and risen Messiah proved from Scripture

schools.summary: The converts were three groups — some Jews, a great multitude of devout Greeks (God-fearers drawn to the God of Israel), and not a few chief women (women of rank); the typical pattern of fewer Jews, more God-fearers, and notable women, founding a mixed church.

themes: some believed, and consorted with Paul and Silas
of the devout Greeks a great multitude
of the chief women not a few
the founding of the Thessalonian church

schools.summary: The unbelieving Jews, moved with envy at Paul's success (especially among the God-fearers), enlisted worthless rabble (agoraioi, idlers and ruffians, Robertson) to incite a mob and assault Jason's house (Paul's host), seeking the apostles; unable to refute Paul, resorting to violence.
Calvin: the perennial pattern — the gospel's success provoking the world's rage and envy.

themes: the Jews moved with envy
lewd fellows of the baser sort, set all the city on an uproar
assaulted the house of Jason
the envious, violent opposition

schools.summary: Not finding Paul and Silas, the mob dragged Jason and brethren before the city rulers (politarchs), charging 'these that have turned the world upside down are come hither also' — a hostile accusation that nonetheless testifies to the gospel's power and spread (Barnes).
Calvin: the charge, meant as reproach, was in truth high praise — the gospel revolutionizing and transforming the world, overturning idolatry and sin.

themes: they drew Jason and certain brethren unto the rulers
these that have turned the world upside down
the testimony to the gospel's power
the world-transforming gospel

schools.summary: The Jews charged the apostles with acting contrary to Caesar's decrees, 'saying that there is another king, one Jesus' — a charge of sedition, distorting Paul's preaching of Jesus as the Messianic king into a political rivalry with Caesar (as the same charge against Christ, Luke 23:2; John 19:12).

themes: contrary to the decrees of Caesar
another king, one Jesus
the political charge of sedition
the parallel with the charge against Christ

schools.summary: The charge of sedition troubled both the people and the city rulers (politarchs), who feared Roman displeasure at the report of 'another king'; the gravity of the treason charge alarming the rulers, responsible to Rome.
Calvin: the providence even in the trouble — the rulers, though troubled, did not condemn the apostles but required only security (v.9), a milder outcome.

themes: they troubled the people and the rulers
the alarm at the charge of treason
the gravity of the political accusation
providential restraint

schools.summary: The rulers took security (a bond, surety) from Jason and the others — a pledge, perhaps that there would be no further disturbance or that Paul would leave — and released the brethren (Robertson); a milder, favorable outcome, the church preserved despite the persecution.

themes: taken security of Jason, and of the other
they let them go
the milder outcome
the church preserved

schools.summary: The brethren, for safety, sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea, where Paul again went to the synagogue — the prudent night departure (Henry) and his regular method; the providence driving Paul to Berea where the noble Bereans awaited (v.11).

themes: sent away Paul and Silas by night unto Berea
went into the synagogue of the Jews
the prudent night departure
persecution driving the gospel forward

schools.summary: The Bereans were more noble in disposition (generous, candid, unprejudiced, Barnes, Ellicott) — receiving the word eagerly yet searching the Scriptures daily to test Paul's teaching; the model of examining the truth, the duty of daily Scripture study, the testing of preaching by the Bible (Barnes); Paul's preaching made Bible students of them (Robertson).

Calvin: not arrogant judgment but examination by the Scripture, the touchstone — refuting the Papist claim that faith rests on church authority, for the Bereans were commended for searching the Scriptures themselves.

debates.question: What does the Bereans' searching the Scriptures establish?

sides.position: The duty and right of private examination — testing all teaching by Scripture, the standard of truth, not resting on church authority

themes: more noble than those in Thessalonica
received the word with readiness, searched the scriptures daily
the model of testing teaching by Scripture
faith and examination balanced

schools.summary: Many Bereans believed as the result of their searching the Scriptures (finding Paul's doctrine accorded with the Old Testament, Barnes), along with honorable Greek women and men; honest examination of the Scriptures commonly leading to faith (Barnes).

themes: therefore many of them believed
of honourable women and of men, not a few
the fruit of Scripture-searching faith
examination leading to conviction

schools.summary: The Thessalonian Jews, hearing of Paul's success at Berea, came there and stirred up the people — pursuing him beyond their own city, using the same tactic of mob agitation as before (v.5); the persistent persecution following the apostle.
Calvin: the perennial enmity — the gospel's success provoking the enemies to follow and oppose it.

themes: the Jews of Thessalonica came to Berea also
stirred up the people
the persistent persecution
the enemies pursuing Paul

schools.summary: The brethren swiftly sent Paul away for safety (as at Thessalonica), directing him toward the sea (the route to Athens), while Silas and Timothy remained at Berea to continue the work; the prudent departure and division of labor, the providence sending Paul to Athens.

themes: the brethren sent away Paul to go to the sea
Silas and Timotheus abode there still
the prudent departure
Paul sent on to Athens

schools.summary: The brethren conducted Paul to Athens (the great center of philosophy and learning) and carried back his command for Silas and Timothy to join him quickly; Paul arriving alone at the intellectual capital, soon to confront the philosophers, summoning his companions for fellowship and the work.

themes: they brought him unto Athens
a commandment unto Silas and Timotheus to come with speed
arrival at the philosophical capital
the summons to his companions

schools.summary: Paul, waiting at Athens, was deeply stirred (provoked, grieved, Robertson, paroxyno) at the city wholly given to idolatry — full of statues, temples, and altars, more gods than men; his holy indignation at the dishonor done to God.
Chrysostom: the contrast — the apostle grieved not at the splendid art but at the idolatry, the souls in darkness; the heart of the evangelist moved by the dishonor of God.

themes: his spirit was stirred in him
the city wholly given to idolatry
holy indignation at the idolatry
grief at spiritual darkness, not admiration of art

schools.summary: Paul reasoned in the synagogue with the Jews and God-fearers (his regular method) and daily in the marketplace (the agora) with whoever he met — adapting to the philosophical city, where Socrates had reasoned with passers-by; the apostle's diligent, adaptable engagement.

themes: disputed in the synagogue and in the market daily
with Jews, devout persons, and whoever he met
the diligent, adaptable engagement
synagogue and marketplace reasoning

schools.summary: The Epicurean (denying providence and a future life, holding pleasure chief, Barnes) and Stoic (fate and self-sufficient virtue, proud) philosophers encountered Paul — the Sadducee and Pharisee of philosophy (Robertson); some scorned him as 'babbler' (spermologos, seed-picker, retailer of scraps), others took 'Jesus and the Anastasis (resurrection)' as two new deities (Barnes, Robertson), the charge that condemned Socrates.

Calvin: the Epicureans open enemies to learning, denying providence and the soul's immortality; the Stoics proud, corrupting providence into fate; both far from godliness, captains in assaulting the gospel.

debates.question: What did the philosophers take 'Jesus and the resurrection' to be?

sides.position: Two new deities — Jesus and the goddess Anastasis (Resurrection); misunderstanding the gospel as new idolatry

themes: the Epicureans and Stoics encountered him
What will this babbler say
setter forth of strange gods
Jesus and the resurrection misunderstood

schools.summary: The philosophers brought Paul to the Areopagus (Mars' Hill, the seat of the ancient Athenian court with jurisdiction over religion, Barnes, Ellicott), asking to know his 'new doctrine' — whether for a formal hearing or a place of discourse is debated (Robertson, Ellicott); no necessary violence, the request courteous yet curious, reflecting the Athenian love of novelty (v.21).

themes: brought him unto Areopagus
May we know what this new doctrine is
the revered Athenian court
the platform for the great sermon

schools.summary: The Athenians, finding Paul's doctrine 'strange' (surprising, shocking, xenizonta), wished to understand it — a polite inquiry driven by their appetite for novelty (v.21); the curiosity inviting Paul's full exposition of the gospel.

themes: thou bringest certain strange things to our ears
we would know what these things mean
the Athenian curiosity
the invitation to expound the gospel

schools.summary: Luke characterizes the Athenians (and foreigners there) as spending their time in nothing but telling or hearing some new thing — the famous Athenian love of novelty, a vain restless curiosity (Calvin), not a genuine desire for truth, attested by ancient writers; explaining the shallow response (vv.32-34).

themes: spent their time to tell or hear some new thing
the Athenian love of novelty
idle curiosity, not earnest seeking
the contrast with the noble Bereans

schools.summary: Paul opened his sermon to the heathens (leading them by providence to the Creator, Henry) by observing the Athenians were very religious; deisidaimonesteros is neutral with a slight touch of disparagement ('more fearful of the gods'), a conciliatory opening to gain their ears (Ellicott, Robertson).

Clarke argues strongly for 'religious,' not 'superstitious' — Paul stating a fact (the Athenians most devoted to religion) to gain a hearing, a captatio benevolentiae, not an insult that would have lost his audience.

debates.question: Does 'too superstitious' mean a rebuke or a tactful acknowledgment of religiousness?

sides.position: A neutral-to-slightly-disparaging acknowledgment ('more fearful of the gods'), a conciliatory opening
'Religious' — a fact stated as a point of contact to gain a hearing, not an insult

themes: Paul stood in the midst of Mars' hill
ye are too superstitious
the masterful conciliatory opening
superstitious vs religious

schools.summary: Paul, having seen an altar 'TO THE UNKNOWN GOD' (a historically attested Athenian practice, from the Epimenides story, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott), used it with consummate skill (Robertson) — proclaiming not a new god (eluding the charge of v.18) but the one they ignorantly worshipped; meeting the Athenians on their own ground, the altar of ignorance the bridge to the true God.
Calvin: Paul did not flatter their worship but took from their confessed ignorance free matter for teaching — God cannot be rightly worshipped unless first made known.

themes: an altar with this inscription, TO THE UNKNOWN GOD
whom ye ignorantly worship, him declare I
the masterful point of contact
natural theology and its limit

schools.summary: Paul declared God the Creator of the world and Lord of heaven and earth, who dwells not in temples made with hands — opposing the Epicurean view (no creation) and polytheism (Barnes, Robertson, echoing Isaiah 42:5), and echoing Stephen's words (7:48) that Saul had once heard (Ellicott).
Calvin: the method — arguing from nature, beginning with the definition of God (the Creator) to establish true worship; knowing the true God is the foundation of right worship.

themes: God that made the world and all things
Lord of heaven and earth
dwelleth not in temples made with hands
the Creator against idolatry

schools.summary: God is not served by men's hands as though needing anything, for he gives to all life, breath, and all things — refuting the heathen notion that the gods need offerings (Barnes); God the Giver who needs nothing, worship for man's benefit not God's need (Clarke).
Calvin: men cannot give God anything, having nothing but what he gives — the argument against ceremonialism, God the self-sufficient Giver.

themes: neither is worshipped with men's hands, as though he needed any thing
he giveth to all life, and breath, and all things
God the self-sufficient Giver
against ritualism

schools.summary: God made of one blood all nations (the unity of the race, striking at racial pride and oppression, Barnes; rising above the Greek/barbarian distinction, Ellicott, Robertson) and determined their appointed times and the bounds of their habitation — God's sovereign providence over history, not blind chance or Stoic fate (Robertson).

Calvin emphasizes God's counsel, not bare foreknowledge — God governs all events by his decree, appointing the times and bounds of every nation; the rise and fall of nations by his appointment, not chance or fate.

debates.question: Does 'determined the times and bounds' mean mere foreknowledge or active decree?

sides.position: Active decree — God's counsel and will governing all history, not chance or Stoic fate

themes: made of one blood all nations of men
determined the times and the bounds of their habitation
the unity of mankind
God's sovereign providence over the nations

schools.summary: The purpose of God's providence is that men should seek him, groping after him (the blind groping of the darkened heathen mind, Robertson, Ellicott) and finding him, for he is not far from any — God placing men in the world to know him (Barnes, Romans 1:19-20); the appeal to conscience, God near in the depths of each being (Ellicott).

Calvin: God offers himself near to all, so men's blindness is inexcusable; yet by nature alone, without the Spirit's illumination, they cannot truly find him, though they remain without excuse for not seeking.

themes: that they should seek the Lord
if haply they might feel after him
God not far from every one of us
the search for God and his nearness

schools.summary: God is so near that in him we live, move, and have our being (real immanence, not Stoic pantheism, Robertson; the three verbs of animal life, emotion, essence, Ellicott); Paul quotes the Greek poets ('we are his offspring,' from Aratus his Cilician countryman, and Cleanthes, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson), using their own writers against idolatry.

Calvin: the poets spoke of Zeus, but Paul applies the true principle — men are God's offspring by creation, made in his image (the natural kinship of all men, not the adoption of believers); the right use of a true maxim though corrupted by pagan fables.

debates.question: In what sense are all men God's 'offspring'?

sides.position: By creation — all men bear God's image, the natural kinship of the race with the Creator, not the adoption of believers

themes: in him we live, and move, and have our being
we are also his offspring
God's immanence
the masterful use of the Greek poets

schools.summary: Since we are God's offspring (made in his image, living and intelligent), we ought not to think the Godhead like gold, silver, or stone, made by human craft — drawn from the poets' own words (Robertson): if man, God's offspring, is more excellent than a statue, how much more the Creator (Barnes).
Calvin: the soul, bearing God's image, cannot be painted; how much more absurd to paint God — the refutation of idolatry from the kinship of man with God.

themes: we are the offspring of God
not to think the Godhead like gold, silver, or stone
the argument against idolatry
the kinship of man with God

schools.summary: God overlooked the times of ignorance (not condoning but not fully punishing the idolatry before the gospel, Robertson, Barnes — a mitigation of guilt, not innocence, Ellicott), but now commands all men everywhere to repent; repentance a duty, possible, binding on all, necessary (Barnes).
Calvin: the forbearance and urgency — God overlooked the past, but now, when he speaks, men must obey without delay; the ignorance of former times excuses no longer.

debates.question: Does God 'winking at' the times of ignorance mean condoning or overlooking?

sides.position: Overlooking, not condoning — a forbearance and mitigation of guilt (not innocence), the idolatry not fully punished before the gospel came

themes: the times of this ignorance God winked at
now commandeth all men every where to repent
God's forbearance
the universal call to repentance

schools.summary: God has appointed a day to judge the world by the man he ordained (Jesus the Judge, Robertson, Matthew 25), giving assurance by raising him from the dead — the reason for the command to repent (all must be judged, Barnes); the resurrection the proof (pistis, a ground of conviction) that Jesus is the Judge, the heart of Paul's message clarifying v.18 (Robertson).
Calvin: Christ by his resurrection is declared the Judge of the world — the resurrection openly showing what God had decreed concerning him; men must obey the appointed Judge.

themes: he hath appointed a day to judge the world
by that man whom he hath ordained
the resurrection as the assurance/proof
the risen Christ the Judge of all

schools.summary: When Paul preached the resurrection, some mocked (the Greek scorn of the bodily resurrection, incredible to the philosophers) and others deferred ('we will hear thee again') — a polite postponement often masking unbelief; the resurrection the stumbling-block to Greek wisdom.
Chrysostom: the point of offense — they listened to talk of God and providence, but the resurrection, the bodily rising, they scorned.

themes: when they heard of the resurrection, some mocked
others said, We will hear thee again
the Greek scorn of the resurrection
mockery and deferral, both unbelief

schools.summary: Paul departed from the Areopagus — a quiet exit, the sermon ended, the response mostly mockery or deferral (Robertson: never to return to Athens); Barnes notes his wisdom in not lingering in a barren field; no condemnation, Paul leaving freely.

themes: so Paul departed from among them
the quiet departure
not lingering in a barren field
the close of the Athens episode

schools.summary: Despite the mockery, certain men clave to Paul and believed — including Dionysius the Areopagite (a member of the revered court, a man of standing worth Paul's labor, Barnes; later tradition's bishop of Athens), Damaris, and others; Ellicott notes the contrast with the 'many' at Thessalonica and Berea, Athens yielding few yet some saved.

themes: certain men clave unto him, and believed
Dionysius the Areopagite, and a woman named Damaris
the small but genuine fruit at Athens
souls saved even where the many mocked
